Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a set number of hours each week, but it rarely worked out that way. I'd often end up cramming information in bursts, usually when anxiety kicked in about an upcoming deadline. I preferred listening to recorded lectures while doing other things around the house, which worked better for me than sitting and reading. At 28, I've got a lot going on outside of school, so structured routines just don't stick for me.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything course-related, and then I'd have intense periods where I'd try to catch up on everything at once. Deadlines definitely drove my engagement more than any internal schedule. If nothing was due soon, I'd drift away from the coursework pretty easily. It's not that I didn't care—I just had trouble maintaining consistent momentum.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scrolling through quickly without really diving deep. Peer messages occasionally pulled me back in too—seeing that classmates were discussing something made me curious. But honestly, if there wasn't an immediate reason, I could go days without thinking about the LMS.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially early in the semester when everything was new and I was more motivated. But that routine never really stuck for long. Once I fell behind on a few things, the habit broke and it became harder to rebuild. The anxiety of seeing incomplete tasks actually made me avoid logging in sometimes, which I know is counterproductive.
Q5: The audio lectures and any recorded discussions were gold for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen while commuting or doing chores, which fit my lifestyle way better than sitting at a computer reading. Discussion boards were helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways—sometimes their explanations clicked better than the official materials. I also appreciated when instructors summarized key points verbally rather than just in text.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just piled up in my downloads folder untouched. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The discussion posts I was supposed to make—I only did two the whole course because writing out my thoughts felt like such a chore compared to just listening and absorbing information.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it directly connected to the exam or final grade. If something seemed like busy work or wasn't clearly tied to assessment, I'd deprioritize it immediately. I also valued activities that I could engage with passively—listening rather than actively producing something. With limited time and high anxiety, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't required, it wasn't happening. I know that sounds bad, but when you're already struggling to complete the mandatory stuff, optional feels like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which honestly was the only thing that got me moving sometimes.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lectures and listened to them repeatedly offline, sometimes while falling asleep or during my morning routine. I had a notebook where I'd jot down key terms and concepts that I'd review independently. I also used YouTube videos and podcasts on related topics that explained things in ways I found easier to understand. A lot of my actual learning happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: There were many times when I was actively studying but the LMS wouldn't show it at all. I'd have downloaded materials open on my tablet, listening to lectures I'd saved, making voice memos to myself about concepts. I'd discuss course content with a friend who was in a similar program, bouncing ideas off each other. None of that showed up anywhere in my digital footprint for the course.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or lecture once, download it, and then replay it multiple times offline over the following days. The LMS would only show one view, but I might have listened to that same content five or six times while doing other activities. It was the most efficient way for me to absorb information given my learning style and schedule.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal for me. My anxiety spiked even though my stress was generally manageable, and I found myself avoiding the course entirely for stretches. The more I fell behind, the more overwhelming it felt to catch up, which created this vicious cycle. Having peer support helped pull me back eventually, but there were definitely weeks where I was just drifting.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor. At 28, I'm juggling more than just school—work, relationships, life stuff. When those demands increased, the LMS was the first thing I neglected. Fatigue also played a role; some evenings I'd log in with good intentions but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely rather than seeking help.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd have videos playing but I was completely zoned out, thinking about other things. I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but nothing was actually sinking in. It was like going through the motions without any real cognitive engagement. Those sessions felt pointless in retrospect.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—I'd overthink how to approach something until the deadline was right on top of me. Then I'd rush through and submit something mediocre. I knew this pattern was hurting my assignment scores, but breaking it felt beyond my control sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage with the LMS seriously. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become intensely focused for a day or two, cramming everything in at the last minute. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned. My exam score being decent shows I could perform under pressure, even if my assignment completion was terrible.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me more likely to engage, but critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and caused me to avoid that area of the course. I appreciated when feedback was constructive and specific, but vague criticism just made me feel lost. The peer support intervention actually helped me process feedback better.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me some framework, but the volume of content across 15 online courses was overwhelming. I appreciated when things were broken into smaller chunks, but when modules were dense, I'd just skip ahead to what seemed most essential for exams. More flexibility in deadlines would have helped someone like me.
Q19: Recorded lectures made engagement easier because they fit my auditory learning style and flexible schedule. What made it harder was the emphasis on written discussion posts and assignments—that's just not how I process or express information best. Also, when the LMS interface was cluttered or confusing, I'd get frustrated and log off rather than figure it out.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I logged in frequently, watched some videos, had peer interactions, but my assignment completion was terrible. For me, it was anxiety about producing work combined with not prioritizing assignments over exam prep. Some people might be exploring, trying to understand material, but freezing when it comes to actually submitting something. The pressure of being evaluated can be paralyzing.
Q21: That also reflects my experience in a way. My exam score was good despite low attendance and mediocre assignment completion. I was learning effectively through methods that didn't show up in the LMS—listening to downloaded content, studying with peers outside the platform, using external resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture the full picture of how someone is actually engaging with the material.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing, and completion of specific tasks. They're good at capturing when you logged in and what you clicked on. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether you were actually focused or just going through motions. They miss offline study, external resources, peer discussions outside the platform, and the emotional experience of learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, engagement happened in bursts—moments when something clicked and I wanted to explore further. It's not about how many times you log in; it's about whether the learning is meaningful to you.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels and how they affected my participation patterns. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while doing dishes or commuting. It wouldn't show the conversations with peers that happened over text or in person, or how the peer support intervention genuinely improved my experience. My internal struggle between wanting to succeed and feeling paralyzed by the workload—none of that shows up in click data.
